**Introduction:**
"Good morning, class! Today, we are going to dive into the exciting world of fractions. We will be exploring improper fractions and mixed numbers. Are you ready to become fraction detectives and solve some fraction mysteries?"

**Warm-Up Activity: Pizza Party**
To start, I will draw a large pizza on the board and divide it into 4 equal slices. I will ask the students, "If we cut the pizza into 4 equal slices, how many slices make a whole pizza?" This will help them understand the concept of fractions as parts of a whole.

**Introducing Improper Fractions:**
Next, I will bring out a chocolate bar kit that is pre-broken into 4 pieces. I will show the students 5 pieces and ask, "Do we have more than a whole? How many whole bars do we see?" We will count together and identify that we have 1 whole bar and 1 piece left over, representing 5/4 as 1 ¼. This will help them understand the concept of improper fractions.

**Guided Modeling with Fraction Strips:**
I will use fraction strips to visually represent 5/4. We will group the strips in fours to show 1 whole and then see the leftover strip. This will help students understand the division process to convert improper fractions to mixed numbers.

**Interactive Fill-the-Pie Game:**
Using an interactive fill-the-pie game on the whiteboard, I will give the students fractions like 9/6 and ask them to place the slices on the pie. We will discuss how many whole pizzas are formed and what is left over. This will reinforce the concept of mixed numbers.

**Partner Practice - Fraction Detective:**
Students will work in pairs to identify improper fractions from a set of cards and convert them into mixed numbers. I will circulate and ask probing questions to deepen their understanding of the concept.

**Mini-Lesson on Notation:**
I will explain the notation for writing mixed numbers, emphasizing the format of whole number + space + fraction. We will practice writing mixed numbers correctly to avoid common errors.

**Independent Practice:**
Students will work on a worksheet with 5 practice problems ranging from 5/4 to 14/6. I will provide support as needed and encourage the use of fraction strips for visual assistance.

**Exit Ticket:**
As a quick check for understanding, students will complete an exit ticket where they have to write 11/4 as a mixed number and draw a picture to represent it. This will assess their ability to apply the concept independently.

**Closing - Think-Pair-Share:**
In the closing, students will think about a real-life scenario involving an improper fraction and share how they would convert it to a mixed number. This will reinforce the practical application of the concept.

**Reflection:**
After the lesson, I will reflect on how well the students understood the concept of improper fractions and mixed numbers. I will assess their ability to convert fractions correctly and address any common misconceptions that may have arisen during the lesson.

By incorporating hands-on activities, visual aids, interactive games, and opportunities for peer collaboration, I aim to make the lesson engaging and accessible for 8-year-old students. This approach will help them develop a solid understanding of fractions and confidently convert improper fractions to mixed numbers.
